_meta.note: Cross-period causal model. annotation_ids reference IDs in annotations_merged.ts. Highlights structural trends not visible in any single-period file.

nodes.label: Gold price surge (2023–2026)
nodes.description: Gold prices nearly doubled in INR terms between 2023 and Feb 2026. Higher collateral values expand loan eligibility and LTV headroom, compounding gold loan volume beyond demand growth alone.

nodes.label: RBI risk-weight hike on unsecured credit (Nov 2023)
nodes.description: RBI raised risk weights on credit cards and consumer credit from 100% to 125% in Nov 2023. This structurally raised the cost of capital for issuers and forced portfolio repricing. Effect fully visible in Jan 2025 → Jan 2026 YoY comparison.

nodes.label: RBI PSL housing limit revision (Oct 2024)
nodes.description: RBI raised PSL-eligible housing loan ceilings: metro from ₹35L to ₹45L, non-metro from ₹25L to ₹35L. Existing on-book loans below new thresholds reclassified as PSL, creating a one-time step-up in reported PSL housing credit.

nodes.label: PLI scheme capex drawdown phase (FY25–26)
nodes.description: Production-Linked Incentive scheme recipients approved in 2022-23 entered the investment phase in FY25-26, driving real capex credit drawdowns in electronics, defence, medical devices, and EV components. Visible as multi-year All Engineering surge in merged data.

nodes.label: MSME formalisation via UDYAM + GST data
nodes.description: UDYAM registration and GST transaction history have progressively made MSMEs underwritable. Banks that built MSME origination infrastructure over 2022-24 are now harvesting — visible as YoY growth tripling (9.6% → 31.2%) in the Jan 2025 → Jan 2026 comparison.

nodes.label: NBFC credit recovery post risk-weight cycle
nodes.description: RBI risk weights on bank-to-NBFC credit (raised 2023) temporarily suppressed flows. By Jan 2026 the channel has fully reopened, with NBFC credit re-accelerating. The January NBFC series is null — only March SIBC captures NBFC sub-classification.

nodes.label: Infrastructure supercycle completing (FY22–24)
nodes.description: Large infrastructure projects funded in the FY22-24 capex wave are now in operations, shifting from drawdown to repayment. The next wave (data centers, green hydrogen, semiconductor fab) has not yet reached credit drawdown scale.

nodes.label: Total Bank Credit
nodes.description: System-level credit aggregate. ₹160.4L Cr (Jan 2024) → ₹178.7L Cr (Jan 2025) → ₹204.8L Cr (Jan 2026). YoY accelerated from 11.4% to 14.6%. FY26 tracking at 13.8% vs FY25's 11.0%.

nodes.label: Industry credit
nodes.description: Industry credit re-accelerating after two years of slower growth. Led by Micro & Small enterprises and All Engineering. Large corporate growth structurally constrained.

nodes.label: Services credit
nodes.description: Services credit growing fast, driven by Computer Software and CRE. NBFCs dominate the category but January SIBC files do not capture NBFC sub-classification.

nodes.label: Personal Loans
nodes.description: Largest sector by outstanding. Internally bifurcated: gold loans and vehicle loans accelerating; credit cards and consumer durables decelerating. The aggregate hides a structural mix shift.

nodes.label: Micro & Small Enterprises
nodes.description: Fastest-growing industry segment. YoY growth tripled from 9.6% (Jan 2025) to 31.2% (Jan 2026). PSL Micro & Small corroborates with +25.6% FY (Feb 2026). Two independent series confirming the same structural acceleration.

nodes.label: Large Corporate credit
nodes.description: Large enterprise credit growing only +5.7% FY (Feb 2026), structurally limited by deleveraged balance sheets and internal accrual funding. No cross-period acceleration visible.

nodes.label: Gold Loans
nodes.description: Most dramatic growth story in the data. ₹0.93L Cr (Mar 2024) → ₹2.06L Cr (Mar 2025) → ₹4.29L Cr (Feb 2026). +359% in 24 months. Only visible in March SIBC files — January data is null for this series.

nodes.label: Credit Cards Outstanding
nodes.description: YoY collapsed from +13.0% (Jan 2025) to +1.5% (Jan 2026). The cross-period comparison makes the deceleration unambiguous. RBI risk-weight hike is the direct cause.

nodes.label: Vehicle Loans
nodes.description: Accelerating counter-trend to credit cards. YoY: +9.7% (Jan 2025) → +17.1% (Jan 2026). EV adoption and premium vehicle financing driving growth beyond pure volume.

nodes.label: All Engineering (Industry by Type)
nodes.description: Fastest-growing large industry sub-sector. ₹1.96L Cr (Feb 2024) → ₹2.32L Cr (Feb 2025) → ₹3.16L Cr (Feb 2026). +62% in 2 years. PLI capex drawdowns driving the surge.

nodes.label: Infrastructure credit
nodes.description: Decelerating at +5.8% FY (Feb 2026). At ₹14.44L Cr and 32.5% of industry credit, the growth rate matters systemically. Projects from FY22-24 capex wave entering operations.

nodes.label: Computer Software & IT Services
nodes.description: Multi-year surge visible across all periods in merged data. Consistent structural growth driven by global IT services expansion and domestic digital infrastructure spend.

nodes.label: PSL Housing
nodes.description: ₹7.55L Cr (Mar 2024) → ₹7.47L Cr (Mar 2025) → ₹10.33L Cr (Feb 2026). Near-doubling in 11 months after contraction. Driven by Oct 2024 PSL limit revision reclassifying existing loans.

nodes.label: Transport Operators
nodes.description: Decelerating cross-period. YoY: +12.0% (Jan 2025) → +4.3% (Jan 2026). Post-COVID logistics credit normalization after the fleet expansion surge of FY24.

nodes.label: January SIBC missing Gold, Housing, NBFC, Tourism data
nodes.description: January SIBC files do not report sub-classifications for Gold Loans, PSL Housing, NBFC credit, and Tourism. These series only appear in March files. Merged Jan→Jan YoY comparisons for these categories are not possible.

nodes.label: Food credit seasonal artifact contaminates totals
nodes.description: Food credit peaks in January (kharif procurement season) and drops by March. This causes the Jan bank credit total to appear larger than March, distorting FY-to-date growth calculations. Non-food credit is the correct signal.

nodes.label: PSL housing growth is reclassification, not new lending
nodes.description: The +38.4% FY growth in PSL Housing (Feb 2026) represents existing loans being reclassified under new PSL ceilings, not new lending. The data shows credit growth that did not occur.

nodes.label: Gold loan market entry / deepening
nodes.description: Banks entering or deepening gold lending are taking share from NBFCs. The product moat is operational (branch-level assay, cash management) not regulatory. The growth trajectory is structural, not cyclical.

nodes.label: MSME origination infrastructure
nodes.description: Banks with MSME origination infrastructure built in 2022-24 are now harvesting. The window is open but will close as competition intensifies. The next ₹10L Cr of industrial credit will come from SMEs.

nodes.label: PLI supply chain finance
nodes.description: Tier-2 and tier-3 suppliers to PLI anchor companies represent the next layer of manufacturing credit. Anchor company relationships provide implicit credit assurance for supply chain finance structures.

nodes.label: EV-specific vehicle credit products
nodes.description: Vehicle loan acceleration is partly EV-driven. EV-specific loan products (battery residual value, charging infrastructure finance) represent a product differentiation opportunity.

nodes.label: Renewable energy project finance
nodes.description: Green hydrogen, solar, and battery storage projects represent the next infrastructure capex wave. Banks with project finance capability for renewable energy will capture the credit as the infrastructure supercycle pivots.

nodes.label: Unsecured credit demand migrating to untracked channels
nodes.description: Credit card and consumer durable credit slowing does not mean demand has disappeared. BNPL, UPI credit lines, and sachet personal loans are absorbing displaced demand in channels not captured by SIBC data.

nodes.label: Infrastructure repayment wave with low new origination
nodes.description: Infrastructure credit books built on the FY22-24 cycle face high repayments and low new drawdowns. Banks with heavy infra exposure need to build capability for the next cycle (renewables, data centers) or face book shrinkage.
